title: Quiz: Quantum Advantage Preview
description: Test your understanding of quantum computing advantages and limitations

questions.question: What speedup does Grover's algorithm provide for searching?
questions.explanation: Grover's algorithm provides quadratic speedup: O(√N) queries instead of O(N). For a million items, this means ~1000 steps instead of 1 million.

questions.question: What type of speedup does Shor's algorithm provide for factoring?
questions.explanation: Shor's algorithm provides exponential speedup: O(n³) quantum vs O(exp(n^⅓)) classical for n-bit numbers. This makes RSA encryption breakable.

questions.question: Quantum computers can efficiently solve all NP-complete problems.
questions.explanation: False! Quantum computers don't efficiently solve NP-complete problems. Grover's algorithm gives only quadratic speedup for search, not enough to make NP-complete problems tractable.

questions.question: What happens when you measure an entangled Bell state |00⟩ + |11⟩?

options.text: Get either 00 or 11, but never 01 or 10

questions.explanation: The Bell state |00⟩ + |11⟩ collapses to either |00⟩ or |11⟩ with 50% probability each. The qubits are perfectly correlated - if one is 0, the other is always 0.

questions.question: Which quantum resource allows exploring many possibilities simultaneously?
questions.explanation: Superposition allows a qubit to be in a combination of |0⟩ and |1⟩ states, enabling quantum parallelism where multiple computational paths are explored simultaneously.

questions.question: What is the main challenge limiting current quantum computers?
questions.explanation: Decoherence (qubits losing their quantum state) and high error rates (~0.1-1% per gate) are the main challenges. This is why we need quantum error correction.

questions.question: Quantum computers are faster than classical computers for all computational tasks.
questions.explanation: False! Quantum computers only provide speedups for specific problems. For most everyday tasks like web browsing, word processing, and simple arithmetic, classical computers are better.

questions.question: What makes quantum computers naturally suited for simulating molecules?
questions.explanation: Molecules are quantum mechanical systems. Simulating them classically requires exponential resources (2^n states), but quantum computers naturally represent quantum states, making simulation efficient.
